title: Drum-leaf album
subtitle: Single leaves joined at alternating edges to fan smoothly to both covers
excerpt: The drum-leaf structure joins individual sheets by gluing alternating fore-edges together, so the book fans open completely flat on either side. It is the natural choice for an artist or photograph album where every page needs to lie fully open.
sourceNotes: The drum-leaf structure is a twentieth-century development documented in bookbinding literature from the 1970s onward. Consistent with structural principles set out in Keith Smith, Non-Adhesive Binding Vol. 1 (1992) and Esther K. Smith, How to Make Books (2007), both post-copyright but treated here as synthesised practice from contemporary craft documentation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A leaf bound by gluing its fore-edge to the fore-edge of its neighbour, alternating so the album fans to either cover without a spine fold.
The open edge of a book opposite the spine, where pages are turned.
The gathered pages of a book before they are placed inside the cover boards.

content.text: A drum-leaf album is a book without a spine fold. Each leaf is a single sheet, and each leaf is joined to its neighbour by gluing a narrow strip of its fore-edge to the narrow strip of the neighbouring leaf's fore-edge, alternating sides as the leaves stack up. The result fans open completely flat in either direction, making every page fully accessible for photographs, pressed botanicals, or artwork. The finished album sits with a rounded, "drummed" fore-edge when closed, which gives the structure its name.

Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing mat against a metal ruler, with the non-cutting hand held well behind the blade's path.
What you need

Pages: cartridge paper, 130 gsm, grain running parallel to the spine (short grain for a portrait-format album). Cut to finished leaf size plus 3 mm on the fore-edge for the gluing tab. For a standard A5 album (148 x 210 mm finished), cut leaves to 151 x 210 mm. Prepare pairs: each page of the album requires two individual leaves.
Covers: bookbinding board, 2 mm. Cut to the same height as the leaves and 5 mm wider at the fore-edge than the text block.
Adhesive: acid-free PVA thinned slightly with water to a consistency that flows but does not drip. A thick PVA causes warping in thin paper.
Marking and scoring the gluing tabs

On each leaf, score a line 3 mm in from the fore-edge using the bone folder against a metal ruler. Score on the face that will receive glue. The score makes the fold crisp rather than bowed, which matters when gluing narrow tabs accurately. Fold each tab under along the score. Keep all tabs folded in the same direction for now.
Assembling the drum
Lay the first leaf face-up on the bench with its gluing tab folded under. Apply a thin, even coat of PVA to the tab.
Place the second leaf face-down on top of the first, aligning its fore-edge with the first leaf's fore-edge. Press firmly along the tab. This join is at the fore-edge; pages 1 and 2 are joined here.
Open the joined pair. Apply PVA to the tab of the second leaf (the one now face-up) on its reverse face.

Bring the third leaf face-up onto the assembly, aligning it at the fore-edge. The join alternates: leaf 1-2 is glued on one side; leaf 2-3 is glued on the other side. This alternation is what creates the drum.
Continue adding leaves, alternating the gluing side each time. After every four or five additions, place the assembly under a light weight for a minute to let the PVA tack before continuing.

Once all leaves are joined, clamp the text block in the book press with the fore-edge proud. The joined fore-edge column should be visible as a row of overlapping tabs. Allow to dry fully, at least 2 hours.
Attaching the covers
The covers attach to the outermost leaves of the
text block
. Apply PVA to the spine-side edge of each board and glue it flush with the spine edge of the first and last leaf. The
fore-edge
of each board should overhang the text block edge by about 5 mm to protect the
drum leaf
joins.
Finishing

Cover the boards with decorative paper or book cloth if desired. Trim any uneven fore-edge tabs with a craft knife and metal ruler once the PVA is fully cured. The finished album should fan fully flat in either direction with no pages pulling against the spine.
Care

Store flat or upright between books of similar size to support the fore-edge. The glued fore-edge tabs are strong when dry, but avoid wetting or flexing the album spine hard, as there is no sewn reinforcement.
Variations

Use heavier watercolour paper (300 gsm) for a painting album; the drum structure handles thicker sheets without difficulty. Insert protective tissue interleaves between pages when the album holds photographs. For a softcover version, substitute card stock (300 gsm) in place of bookbinding board.
